risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: The allocation sequence was generated by a computer-based randomization program, which is a truly random method. The fact that it was urn randomization with stratification/minimization variables does not reduce confidence in random sequence generation because the method of sequence generation is explicitly described as computer-generated.

Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: Although the text does not explicitly use the words 'allocation concealment,' the use of a computer-generated urn randomization program implies an automated assignment process that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next allocation, and no open list, alternation, or envelope-based procedure is described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All participants received twice weekly group sessions... delivered by instructors experienced in MT... or certified in FFS respectively" and "FFS was chosen as an active ‘standard treatment’ comparison condition". Comment: The report describes active group interventions with different content and different therapists, but it does not state that participants or personnel were blinded; because this was a behavioral trial, blinding was likely not feasible. However, for smoking outcomes the paper does not provide enough direct information to determine whether knowledge of assignment plausibly influenced the reported outcomes across the study overall, so the safest judgment is unclear risk.

Quote: "Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB)... Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement of ≤ 10 parts per million at each of the twice-weekly treatment and at follow-up visits." Comment: The outcome assessor was a research assistant not involved in treatment delivery, but the paper does not state that this assessor was blinded to allocation. The outcome was a mix of patient self-report (where the participant is effectively the assessor) and objective CO verification; because blinding of outcome assessment is not explicitly reported and the primary smoking-status measure includes self-report, the risk of detection bias cannot be judged confidently.

Quote: "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." Comment: Follow-up completion was high and fairly balanced across groups at all reported time points, with no obvious signal that missing data were related to the outcome. The analysis also used an ITT mixed-effects approach on the full randomized sample, and the authors noted that participants who dropped out were contacted for follow-up assessments, which further reduces concern that attrition biased the results.
